original_abstract: The Jesuits, among the most influential missionary organizations in history, maintained a significant presence in the Sonoran Desert—a transborder region now encompassing the Mexican state of Sonora and the U.S. state of Arizona. Following the foundational work of the pioneer Padre Eusebio Kino (d. 1711), the early eighteenth century saw an influx of German-speaking missionaries who expanded this vast evangelical network. Beyond their proselytizing efforts, these individuals served as meticulous scholars of the frontier, producing an extensive corpus of records documenting their scientific observations, cultural encounters, and daily experiences. Although the Order faced expulsion in 1767 and a global suppression in 1773, their legacy remains preserved in these rich archival materials. Today, these missionary accounts serve as vital historical, anthropological, and scientific primary sources, offering profound insights into the colonial encounter and the development of the North American Borderlands.
humanized_abstract: One of the most influential missionary orders in the world, the Jesuits have had a profound legacy in the world’s most remote corners. And though we think of the Jesuits as being far away in exotic lands, they had a considerable mission in the Sonoran Desert of the Americas. This enormous transborder region spans parts of the Mexican state of Sonora and the U.S. state of Arizona. The pioneering work of the missionary Padre Eusebio Kino (d. 1711) was soon followed by a large number of German-speaking Jesuit missionaries during the early eighteenth century. In addition to their missionary work, the various members of the order recorded in great detail their scientific observations of the area, their encounters with native people and the many day-to-day events that occurred within the region of their mission. The Jesuits were eventually expelled from Mexico and the rest of the Americas in 1767 and were later banned by the Pope in 1773, but the vast record of their activities and observations in the Sonoran region remain. Today the written and illustrated records of these Jesuit missionaries serve as some of the most valuable and reliable historical, anthropological and scientific primary source materials for the study of the colonial period of North America and the Greater North American Borderlands.
